does: This paper introduces Generalized Associative Recall (GAR), a synthetic benchmark designed to systematically evaluate and understand compositional relational reasoning in large language models. The benchmark integrates and generalizes tasks from mechanistic interpretability research into a unified framework. Through evaluation on models like Vicuna-33B, the authors demonstrate that existing LLMs struggle with GAR tasks, revealing fundamental deficiencies in compositional relational reasoning capabilities. Using attribution patching and intervention experiments, they identify core circuits and vital attention heads that are reused across different tasks. Crucially, they discover two classes of attention heads whose activations represent abstract notions of true and false in GAR tasks, and show through ablation that proper functioning of these heads significantly impacts overall task performance.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in mechanistic interpretability research that seek to understand the internal operations of transformer models through techniques like circuit analysis and attention head attribution. It responds to growing interest in understanding whether and how LLMs achieve human-like compositional reasoning, connecting to broader questions about compositional generalization in neural networks. The paper synthesizes insights from multiple mechanistic interpretability tasks into a unified benchmark, extending prior work that examined individual aspects of relational reasoning. It leverages established interpretability methods including attribution patching and intervention analysis, which have been developed in parallel streams of research investigating transformer decision-making and the role of specific neural components in task performance.
enables: This work enables deeper investigation into the mechanistic foundations of relational reasoning in LLMs by providing a systematic benchmark and interpretability toolkit. The identified true/false head abstractions suggest avenues for enhancing compositional reasoning through targeted training or architectural modifications. The framework could support research into improving LLM generalization on compositional tasks through circuit-level interventions. Future work could extend GAR to more complex relational reasoning scenarios and investigate how the discovered circuits scale with model size. The identification of reusable core circuits across tasks opens possibilities for studying transfer learning and curriculum learning approaches in mechanistic interpretability, potentially informing new methods for building more interpretable and reliable LLMs with stronger compositional capabilities.
